title: Quiz: Real-Time Fraud Detection Systems
description: Test your understanding of real-time fraud detection architectures

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a cascading model architecture in fraud detection?

options.text: To use multiple identical models for redundancy
To balance latency and accuracy by using fast models first, then slower accurate models for uncertain cases

questions.explanation: Cascading architectures use fast, lightweight models to handle clear-cut cases quickly, while routing uncertain transactions to more sophisticated (but slower) models. This balances the latency requirements with detection accuracy.

questions.question: What is the typical latency requirement for real-time fraud detection in payment systems?
questions.explanation: Payment fraud detection typically requires sub-100ms latency to avoid impacting user experience. Customers expect near-instantaneous transaction processing, so fraud checks must complete within this tight window.

questions.question: How do streaming feature stores handle real-time feature computation?

options.text: By recomputing all features from scratch for each transaction
By maintaining incremental aggregates that update with each event
By using batch processing every minute

questions.explanation: Streaming feature stores maintain running aggregates (counts, sums, averages) in time-bucketed windows. Each new transaction incrementally updates these aggregates, enabling fast feature computation without scanning historical data.

questions.question: What is time bucketing in streaming feature computation?

options.text: Grouping events into fixed time intervals for efficient aggregation

questions.explanation: Time bucketing groups events into fixed intervals (e.g., 1-hour buckets) allowing efficient sliding window aggregates. Instead of scanning all events, the system combines pre-computed bucket summaries to calculate features like 'transactions in last 24 hours'.

questions.question: In hybrid quantum-classical pipelines, quantum models should be used for all transactions to maximize accuracy.
questions.explanation: False. Quantum inference is currently slower and more expensive than classical models. Hybrid architectures use classical models for most transactions and only invoke quantum models for high-value or uncertain cases where the added accuracy justifies the latency cost.

questions.question: What indicates model drift in a production fraud detection system?

options.text: Degradation in detection performance as fraud patterns evolve

questions.explanation: Model drift occurs when the statistical properties of fraud patterns change over time, causing model performance to degrade. Fraudsters constantly adapt their tactics, so models must be monitored and retrained to maintain effectiveness.

questions.question: What is a fallback strategy when the quantum backend is unavailable?

options.text: Reject all transactions until quantum is restored
Use a classical shadow model that approximates quantum predictions

questions.explanation: Production systems maintain classical shadow models trained to approximate quantum model predictions. When quantum backends are unavailable, the system fails over to these classical alternatives to maintain service continuity.

questions.question: Which metrics should be monitored in a real-time fraud detection system? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Critical monitoring includes latency percentiles (P95/P99) to ensure SLA compliance, false positive rates to track model accuracy, and feature distribution shifts to detect data drift. Developer activity is not relevant to model performance.
